1: "Sarah lived to be a hundred and twenty-seven years old (meʾāh šānāh wĕʿeśrîm šānāh wĕšeḇaʿ šānîm)" — Sarah is the only woman in the OT whose age at death is given. The recording of her years with the same detailed formula used for the patriarchs' ages (the years are listed in three groupings — a distinctive pattern) dignifies her as a covenant-figure of equal status. She is 90 at Isaac's birth (Genesis 17:17); Isaac is therefore 37 when Sarah dies. The age-recording places Sarah within the covenant-genealogy framework that defines the patriarchal period as a specific historical span.
2: "She died at Kiriath Arba (that is, Hebron) in the land of Canaan" — Kiriath Arba (qiryat ʾarbaʿ, "city of four") is the pre-Israelite name for Hebron; Joshua 14:15 identifies Arba as "the greatest man among the Anakites." Hebron (from ḥāḇar, to join/be a companion) becomes the patriarchal home-base and burial site. "Abraham went to mourn (lispōd) for Sarah and to weep (liḇkôt) over her" — the two verbs distinguish the formal mourning rite (sāpad, to wail/beat oneself — the formal mourning ceremony) from the personal grief (bākāh, to weep). Abraham performs both the social obligation and the personal grief.
3: "Abraham rose from beside his dead wife and spoke to the Hittites" — the physical detail of "rising from beside his dead wife (mēʿal pĕnê mētô)" depicts the initial mourning position: sitting with the body, then rising to attend to burial practicalities. The transition from grief to action is the ANE burial protocol: the period of initial mourning (sitting beside the dead) was followed by the practical necessity of rapid burial (in the heat of the ANE, burial within 24 hours was medically necessary). The Hittites of Hebron are the local population with whom Abraham must negotiate for burial ground.
4: "I am a foreigner and stranger (gēr-wĕtôšāḇ) among you" — Abraham's formal self-identification in the legal negotiation. The double term gēr-wĕtôšāḇ appears together only here, in Leviticus 25:47, and in Psalms 39:12. The gēr is a resident alien without land rights; the tôšāḇ is a temporary settler. Together they describe Abraham's permanent outsider status: he has lived in Canaan for decades but owns no land. The purchase of the Machpelah cave is the first moment of legal land-ownership in the promised land — the covenant-patriarch buys a grave before he can inhabit the inheritance.
5: The Hittites' response — "listen to us" (šĕmaʿēnû) — initiates the formal ANE negotiation protocol. The instruction to "listen to us" before the offer establishes the public, deliberative character of the gate-council transaction. The Hittite community's willingness to negotiate with Abraham reflects his regional status — he is a stranger but a "mighty prince (nĕśîʾ ʾĕlōhîm, prince of God/a great prince)" in their estimation. The Gerar episode (Genesis 20:15: "my land is before you"), the treaty with Abimelech (Genesis 21:22-23), and Abraham's military reputation (Genesis 14) have established him as a significant regional figure despite his gēr status.
6: "You are a mighty prince (nĕśîʾ ʾĕlōhîm) among us. Bury your dead in the choicest of our tombs" — the Hittite council's generous offer would solve Abraham's immediate problem (Sarah's burial) but without creating legal title. In the ANE, permission to use someone else's tomb was a social concession, not a property transfer; the heirs of the tomb's owner could later reclaim it. Abraham's insistence on purchasing rather than borrowing a tomb (vv8-9,13) reflects his understanding that a covenant-heir must have legal title to a portion of the promised land, not merely social permission to use it temporarily.
7: "Abraham rose and bowed down (wayyiqqad ʾaḇrāhām lipnê ʿam-hāʾāreṣ) before the people of the land" — the prostration before the Hittite council is the standard protocol of respect before the local authority whose approval is needed. "The people of the land (ʿam-hāʾāreṣ)" — a technical term for the established landowning citizens of the territory; the council that has authority to ratify property transfers. Abraham's bow is a social-legal gesture, not worship — the same gesture used when approaching a superior for a legal transaction (cf. Ruth 2:10; 2 Samuel 14:4).
8: "If you are willing to let me bury my dead (ʾim yēš ʾet-napšĕkem liqbōr ʾet-mētî)" — literally "if it is in your soul/desire to bury my dead." The conditional framing of the request is the standard ANE negotiation protocol: the petitioner phrases the request as a matter of the grantor's willingness, not as a demand. "Intercede with Ephron (pĕgĕʿû-bî bĕʿeprôn)" — Abraham requests the council to mediate with the specific landowner rather than negotiating directly; using the community council as intermediary was the formal land-purchase protocol in the ANE city-gate system.
9: "So he will sell me the cave of Machpelah (mĕʿārat hammaḵpēlāh)" — Machpelah may derive from kāpal (to double) — "double cave" — perhaps referring to a two-chambered natural cave formation. "At the end of his field" — a specific location within the larger Ephron property; Abraham is not asking for the whole field but for the specific cave and its immediate surroundings. "For the full price (bĕkesep māleʾ, at full silver/money)" — Abraham explicitly requests to pay full market value, not a discounted charitable price; the full-price insistence is the legal key to creating unambiguous title.
10: "Ephron the Hittite was sitting among his people (yōšēḇ bĕtôk bĕnê-ḥēt)" — the description of Ephron's position within the Hittite community establishes his civic standing; he is a full member of the community with legal authority to conduct property transactions. "He replied to Abraham in the hearing of all the Hittites who had come to the gate of his city (kōl bāʾê šaʿar ʿîrô)" — the city gate as the ANE legal forum: land transactions required public witnesses at the city gate (Ruth 4:1-11; Deuteronomy 25:7-9). The multiple witnesses make the transaction legally binding and publicly documented.
11: "I give you the field, and I give you the cave that is in it" — Ephron's public "gift" is the standard ANE negotiation opening: appearing to give everything for free while actually naming a price in the next breath (v15). The triple "I give" (nĕtattiha... nĕtattîha... nĕtattiha) sounds generous but functions as a negotiating position. "In the presence of my people (lĕʿênê bĕnê-ʿammî) I give it to you" — the public nature of the offer is part of its legal function; the witnesses ensure that what was said is on record. Abraham's response (v13) correctly reads this as a price-setting gesture, not an actual gift.
12: "Again Abraham bowed down (wayyištaḥû ʾaḇrāhām lipnê ʿam-hāʾāreṣ) before the people of the land" — the repeated bow acknowledges Ephron's public generosity while positioning Abraham to make the counter-proposal of actual payment. The reiterated bow maintains the social-honor framework of the negotiation: Abraham is not challenging Ephron's generosity but is insisting that the relationship be structured on legal purchase rather than social obligation. The bow between transactions marks each stage of the formal process.
13: "I will pay the price of the field (nĕtatî kesef haśśādeh)" — Abraham's insistence on paying transforms the social-obligation frame (Ephron's "gift") into a legal-title frame (Abraham's "purchase"). In ANE property law, a gift could be recalled by the donor's heirs; a sale created permanent legal title in the buyer. Abraham's insistence on "paying the price" is not mere scrupulousness but a strategic legal decision: he needs unambiguous, irrecusable title to the first piece of land in the promised inheritance, not a revocable social concession.
14: Ephron's response — "listen to me (šĕmaʿēnî)" — mirrors Abraham's opening formula (v8) and the Hittites' opening (v6): the "listen to me" framing is the formal speech-marker for a statement with legal weight in the ANE negotiation context. The response to Abraham's insistence on payment is a return to the negotiation, now on Abraham's terms: Ephron will name a price. The interplay of "give" language and "buy" language across the negotiation maps the transition from social courtesy to legal transaction.
15: "The land is worth four hundred shekels of silver (ʾarbaʿ-meʾôt šeqel-kesep) — but what is that between you and me?" — the dismissive "what is that between you and me?" is the ANE negotiator's technique of naming a large price while minimizing its apparent significance. 400 shekels of silver is a substantial sum; for comparison, David's purchase of the Araunah threshing floor (the future Temple site) cost 50 shekels (2 Samuel 24:24) or 600 shekels of gold (1 Chronicles 21:25 — different metals, different transaction). The patriarchal silver standard and the later Davidic transaction involve different commodities and different periods; the Machpelah price is consistent with known Nuzi tablet land-purchase values for comparable property.
16: "Abraham agreed to Ephron's terms and weighed out for him the price he had named... four hundred shekels of silver, according to the weight current among the merchants (ʿōḇēr laṣṣōḥēr)" — the legal precision of the payment description establishes the transaction's formal validity. Silver was weighed rather than coined in pre-monetary ANE commerce (coinage was not invented until ca. 7th century BCE in Lydia); the weighing "according to the weight current among the merchants" specifies the standard used — a recognized commercial weight standard rather than a private measure. The public weighing at the city gate, according to the standard measure, in the hearing of all witnesses, is the ANE equivalent of a notarized transaction.
17: "Ephron's field in Machpelah near Mamre — both the field and the cave in it, and all the trees within the borders of the field — was deeded (wayyāqom)" — the deed description's specificity mirrors ANE property-transfer documents. The Nuzi tablets and Egyptian deeds consistently itemize: the field, the structures on the field, and the trees within the field boundaries (trees were separately valuable as long-lived agricultural assets). The completeness of the inventory — field + cave + all trees within borders — is the legal documentation of everything transferred. "Was deeded (wayyāqom, was established/ratified)" — the legal standing (qûm) of the transaction is its irrevocability.
18: "To Abraham as his property (lĕʾaḇrāhām lĕmiqnāh), in the presence of all the Hittites who had come to the gate of the city" — the deed is formally concluded. Miqnāh (purchased property/acquisition) from qānāh (to buy/acquire) is the technical legal term for purchased as opposed to inherited or gifted property. The witnesses — "all the Hittites who had come to the gate" — constitute the public record. In a pre-writing or pre-formal-deed culture, mass witnessing at the city gate was the mechanism of legal record: the community's memory was the title document. The entire Hittite community who witnessed the transaction can testify to Abraham's legal title.
19: "Afterward Abraham buried his wife Sarah in the cave in the field of Machpelah near Mamre (which is at Hebron) in the land of Canaan" — the burial closes the transaction. The purpose of the legal process is fulfilled: Sarah is buried with legal title in the land promised to Abraham's descendants. Machpelah becomes the patriarchal tomb: Abraham himself (Genesis 25:9-10), Isaac and Rebekah (Genesis 49:31), Jacob and Leah (Genesis 49:31; Genesis 50:13) will all be buried here. The cave of Machpelah near Mamre/Hebron is one of the few sites whose patriarchal association is maintained in continuous tradition; the Cave of the Patriarchs in modern Hebron (the Ibrahimi Mosque) is identified with this site.
20: "The field and the cave in it were deeded (wayyāqom) to Abraham by the Hittites as a burial site" — the closing legal summary reiterates the transaction's legal validity. The redundancy is deliberate: this is the only land Abraham legally owns in the promised land during his lifetime, and its legal standing must be unambiguous. The purchase of a burial cave as the first tangible possession of the promised land is one of the OT's most poignant ironies: the covenant-heir owns a grave in the land his descendants will inherit. Stephen cites this in his Acts 7 speech (Acts 7:16 — with some differences): the patriarchs owned no land in the promise except a burial site.
